hw23CTQAAAAJ.keywords: Computational Seismology, Bayesian Inference, Digital Twins for Geological Carbon Storage, Curvelet Transforms, Compressed Sensing, Full-Waveform Inversion (FWI), Sparsity-Promoting Inversion, PDE-Constrained Optimization, Randomized Sampling and Dimensionality Reduction, High-Performance Finite-Difference Solvers (Devito)

mHwonDEAAAAJ.keywords: Computer Networking, Network Algorithmics, Data Streaming Algorithms, Performance Evaluation, Flow Measurement, Sketching and Bloom Filters, Entropy Estimation, IP Traceback and DDoS Mitigation, Traffic Matrix Estimation, Syslog Analysis and Failure Prediction
mHwonDEAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Jun Xu is a **Professor of Computer Science** in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group focused on systems and measurement for modern networks (see his homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~jx). His work sits at the intersection of **computer networking**, **network algorithmics**, and **data streaming algorithms**, with primary emphasis on practical, scalable techniques for high-speed network *measurement*, *security*, and *performance evaluation*. Across his career he has developed compact data structures and streaming methods for online network monitoring, as well as defenses and forensic tools for network security; key recurring themes in his work include <u>flow-size estimation</u>, <u>entropy-based anomaly detection</u>, and <u>IP traceback / DDoS mitigation</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Jun Xu’s research spans several tightly connected areas. In **network measurement and data streaming** he has developed space- and time-efficient algorithms and data structures (e.g., Bloom-filter variants, sketching, and counter architectures) for per-flow measurement, flow size distribution estimation and entropy estimation in high-speed links — problems explored in his papers on the *space-code bloom filter* (2003), *flow size distribution* estimation (2004), and *entropy estimation* (2006). In **network security and forensics** he has contributed practical traceback and packet-filtering techniques for mitigating Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks and for locating prefix hijackers (work on IP traceback and intelligent packet filtering, 2003–2008). He has also addressed **privacy-preserving data sharing**, proposing prefix-preserving anonymization schemes and evaluating their security tradeoffs (2001–2004). More recently his work extends to **datacenter reliability and syslog mining**, applying machine learning and scalable parsing to predict and diagnose switch failures (2017–2018). Underlying these topics is a consistent focus on rigorous performance evaluation and the design of algorithms that meet the stringent space/time constraints of real network devices.

---

## Notable Works
- Space-code bloom filter for efficient traffic flow measurement (2003) — introduced a compact Bloom-filter based technique for accurate per-flow measurement at high speeds; foundational for subsequent sketching and sampling methods in traffic monitoring.
- Data streaming algorithms for efficient and accurate estimation of flow size distribution (2004) and related streaming work — developed algorithms that enable accurate characterization of flow-size distributions and related metrics (e.g., for traffic engineering and anomaly detection) using limited memory and one-pass processing.
- Prefix-preserving IP address anonymization: measurement-based security evaluation and a new cryptography-based scheme (2002/2004) — analyzed privacy risks in sharing network traces and proposed practical anonymization techniques that balance utility for measurement with protection of sensitive information.

---

## Academic Background
Jun Xu is a long-standing faculty member in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he teaches and supervises graduate and undergraduate students and directs research in network systems and algorithms. His publication record (total citations ~7,080) shows sustained impact across measurement, security, and algorithmic design for networks from the late 1990s to the present. His contributions have been published at leading conferences and journals in networking and algorithms, and his work has informed both academic research and practical systems used for network monitoring and defense. He has collaborated widely across the networking community, advised multiple Ph.D. students, and his research has been recognized through high citation counts and repeated presence in top-tier venues. More information, publications, and links to his group can be found on his homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~jx.

mqpjAt4AAAAJ.keywords: Artificial Intelligence, Computer Vision, Deep Learning, Machine Learning, Domain Adaptation, Adversarial Domain Adaptation, Transfer Learning, Sim-to-Real Transfer, Representation Learning, Vision Transformers
mqpjAt4AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Judy Hoffman is an **Associate Professor** at **UC Irvine**, where she leads a research group focused on building robust visual learning systems that generalize across changing environments. Her lab concentrates on *representation learning*, *transfer and domain adaptation*, and the development of benchmarks and datasets that expose real-world distribution shifts. Her work combines adversarial and self-supervised techniques to produce <u>domain-invariant</u> and <u>transferable visual representations</u>, and addresses practical gaps between simulation and reality, multi-modal supervision, and fairness in perception systems.

---

## Research Areas
Hoffman's research sits at the intersection of **computer vision**, **deep learning**, and **machine learning**, with an emphasis on methods that enable models trained on one distribution to perform reliably on another. She has advanced adversarial approaches to adaptation (e.g., "Adversarial discriminative domain adaptation" (2017)) and introduced cycle-consistent image- and feature-level adaptation strategies ("Cycada" (2018)) to close the gap between synthetic and real data. Earlier work demonstrated the broad utility of deep convolutional activations as generic features ("Decaf" (2013)), while a string of subsequent papers developed methods for maximizing <u>domain invariance</u> ("Deep domain confusion" (2014)), multi-source and continuous-shift adaptation, and label-efficient transfer across tasks. Her outputs also include practical benchmarks and challenges (the VisDA datasets and challenge) to drive progress on synthetic-to-real transfer, efforts on pixel-level adaptation for dense prediction ("FCNs in the wild" (2016)), cross-modal supervision transfer, and more recent contributions addressing transformer efficiency and large-scale multimodal video benchmarks (e.g., Token Merging, Ego-Exo4D). Beyond robustness and adaptation, she has probed the social implications of vision systems through work on predictive inequity in object detection.

## Notable Works
- "Adversarial discriminative domain adaptation" (2017) — introduced a practical adversarial framework that separates feature extraction and discriminator training to produce robust cross-domain feature alignments, widely adopted in unsupervised domain adaptation research.
- "Decaf: A deep convolutional activation feature for generic visual recognition" (2013) — provided systematic evidence that deep convolutional activations learned on large-scale supervised tasks can be re-purposed to a broad set of downstream visual recognition problems, helping establish transfer learning as a foundational practice in vision.
- "Cycada: Cycle-consistent adversarial domain adaptation" (2018) — combined cycle-consistent image translation with adversarial and feature-level adaptation to align both pixel and semantic spaces, enabling stronger synthetic-to-real adaptation for classification and segmentation.

## Academic Background
Professor Hoffman has built a prolific research program in visual recognition and domain adaptation, with over 37,000 citations to date and a body of work that spans theory, methodology, datasets, and system evaluation. Her publication record includes highly cited conference and journal contributions, leadership in dataset and benchmark creation (VisDA, Syn2Real, Ego-Exo4D), and collaborative projects spanning robotics, multimodal learning, and fairness in vision. At UC Irvine she directs ongoing projects and graduate students exploring robust perception, while maintaining active collaborations across academic and industrial research labs. Her research impact is reflected in frequent appearances at top-tier venues (CVPR, ICCV, NeurIPS) and in community resources (toolboxes and benchmarks) that have shaped best practices for adapting and evaluating visual models. For more details on publications and current projects, see her homepage: http://judyhoffman.io/.

nfkH5V4AAAAJ.keywords: security, systems, networking, machine learning, applied cryptography, intrusion detection, anomaly detection, botnet detection, malware analysis, virtual machine introspection
nfkH5V4AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Wenke Lee is a **Regents' Professor** and the **John P. Imlay Jr. Chair** in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a long-standing research group associated with the Georgia Tech Information Security Center (GTISC). His primary research focus spans *computer security, systems, and networking*, with deep integration of **machine learning** and elements of **applied cryptography** to solve practical security problems. Over a multi-decade career he has driven foundational work on <u>intrusion detection</u>, <u>botnet and malware detection</u>, <u>virtual machine introspection and secure monitoring</u>, and more recently on the security implications of generative models and deepfakes.

---

## Research Areas
Wenke Lee’s research combines algorithmic and systems approaches to security. Early and highly cited work developed a data-mining paradigm for building adaptive intrusion detection systems, emphasizing systematic feature construction, information-theoretic measures, and cost-sensitive modeling (e.g., the 1998–2001 body of work on data mining for intrusion detection, feature construction, and anomaly detection). He extended these ideas to network-scale threats, producing influential papers on botnet detection (e.g., clustering-based, protocol-agnostic approaches such as BotMiner and flow/behavioral clustering), DNS and DGA-based detection and reputation systems, and temporal/epidemiological analyses of bot propagation. On the systems side, his group advanced practical malware analysis and monitoring techniques using virtualization (Ether, Virtuoso, Lares) and static vetting for mobile platforms (Chex). Methodologically, his group has employed and advanced clustering, ensemble and one-class learning, information-theoretic metrics, causal/plan recognition for alert correlation, and adversarial thinking (e.g., polymorphic and trigger-based evasion), while applying these tools to domains such as MANET security, payload-based anomaly detection, and the detection of synthetic media (deepfakes).

---

## Notable Works
- "Data mining approaches for intrusion detection" (1998) — Established a **data-mining framework for intrusion detection**, introducing systematic feature discovery and model-building for adaptive IDSs; this work seeded a decade of research on automated and scalable anomaly/misuse detection.
- "BotMiner: clustering analysis of network traffic for protocol-and structure-independent botnet detection" (2008) — Introduced a **protocol- and structure-agnostic** clustering method to identify botnets by correlating host and network-level behaviors, shifting detection away from brittle signature and protocol-specific approaches.
- "The creation and detection of deepfakes: A survey" (2021) — Synthesized advances in generative media and defenses, framing the security, privacy, and societal risks of synthetic audiovisual content and outlining detection directions that bridge machine learning and systems security.

---

## Academic Background
Wenke Lee has built his career at Georgia Tech’s College of Computing, holding the Regents' Professorship and the John P. Imlay Jr. Chair and directing a large, interdisciplinary research effort in applied security and systems. With a research portfolio that spans from foundational machine-learning–based intrusion detection to modern problems such as DGA detection, virtualization-based monitoring, mobile app vetting, and deepfake analysis, he is among the most-cited researchers in security (total citations: **52,093**), reflecting sustained impact across academia, industry, and government. His work has informed deployed defenses, multiple patents, and collaborations with operational networks and security vendors. Over the years he has advised numerous Ph.D. students and postdocs and has been recognized through leadership roles, invited keynotes and panels, and prominent service to the security research community.

nuiUbpsAAAAJ.keywords: Networking, Computer Networks, Systems, Mobile Computing, Device-free passive localization, WLAN localization, Congestion control, Traffic shaping, Packet scheduling, LEO satellite networks
nuiUbpsAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Ahmed Saeed is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of Computer Science at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group at the intersection of **computer networks** and **systems**. His work spans practical systems and theory-driven analysis with an emphasis on *scalable, reliable networked systems* and *mobile sensing*. Across his portfolio he has developed and evaluated techniques for <u>device-free passive (DfP) localization</u>, <u>end-host traffic shaping and scheduling</u>, and <u>robust congestion and overload control for datacenter and wide-area traffic</u>. His research blends experimental testbeds, protocol and system design, and empirical modeling to address real-world networking challenges.

---

## Research Areas
Saeed’s research covers several tightly connected areas in networking and systems. In wireless and mobile sensing he has advanced <u>device-free passive localization</u> and motion-detection methods that use existing WLAN infrastructure (e.g., "Nuzzer" (2012), "RASID" (2012), and "Ichnaea" (2013)). In end-host and datacenter systems he has developed scalable mechanisms for traffic shaping and scheduling—both in software and at end hosts—through work such as "Carousel: Scalable traffic shaping at end hosts" (2017) and the family of papers and patents on time-indexed data structures for rate limiting (2018–2019). Saeed has also contributed to congestion control and overload resilience, proposing new control loops and overload controllers that target tail latency and mixed WAN/datacenter workloads (e.g., "Annulus" (2020), "Breakwater" (2020), and "Protego" (2023)). Complementing systems design, he has explored modeling and formal reasoning for network control (e.g., "Toward formally verifying congestion control behavior" (2021)) and studied emerging connectivity platforms from white-space spectrum detection and geo-databases to **LEO satellite networks** and UAV-enabled sensing ("Argus" (2017), LEO network characterization papers 2023–2025). His work frequently couples algorithmic design, experimental evaluation, and practical deployment considerations.

---

## Notable Works
- "Nuzzer: A large-scale device-free passive localization system for wireless environments" (2012) — introduced scalable techniques to localize and track entities that do not carry devices using commodity WLAN infrastructure; a foundational contribution to DfP localization with broad follow-on work and high community impact.
- "Carousel: Scalable traffic shaping at end hosts" (2017) — proposed end-host traffic shaping primitives and data structures that enable policy-driven bandwidth allocation and packet pacing at scale, influencing how end hosts manage high-volume and multi-tenant traffic.
- "Annulus: A Dual Congestion Control Loop for Datacenter and WAN Traffic Aggregates" (2020) — identified interactions between WAN and datacenter traffic that harm tail latency and proposed a dual-loop control design to isolate and mitigate those effects, advancing the state of the art in aggregate congestion control.

---

## Academic Background
Ahmed Saeed’s publication record dates to the early 2010s, with highly cited contributions to wireless localization and mobile systems that formed the basis of his early-career scholarship. He completed his formative research in the early 2010s (with prominent 2012–2013 DfP localization papers) and is now an assistant professor at Georgia Tech’s College of Computing. Earlier work lists collaborations and affiliations with international research centers (e.g., a 2010 DNIS paper connected to Nile University), indicating a career that spans academic and collaborative research appointments. Saeed is an active principal investigator and collaborator on multi-institution research projects (including NSF-style "CNS Core" collaborative efforts reflected in his publication list), and his work has accumulated over 1,900 citations, reflecting sustained community recognition. He maintains an active group webpage (https://www.cc.gatech.edu/~amsmti3) and continues to supervise students, publish in top networking and systems venues, and secure competitive research support.

of5e53cAAAAJ.keywords: human-computer interaction,usability,user interface design,human factors,human performance modeling,monitoring and control,fault diagnosis,large-scale systems,cognitive modeling,usability evaluation
of5e53cAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Richard Henneman is a researcher and educator in the **College of Computing** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, with ties to the *MSHCI program* (homepage: https://mshci.gatech.edu/). His work centers on the study and design of interactive systems with a strong emphasis on <u>usability</u>, <u>human performance</u> in complex operational environments, and the translation of human factors methods into practical design practice. He combines empirical experimentation, formal modeling, and applied design guidance to address how people monitor, diagnose, and control large-scale and transaction-focused systems, and how HCI education and practice can be structured to produce usable, cost-justified solutions.

---

## Research Areas
Henneman’s scholarship spans several interlocking areas of **human-computer interaction** and human factors. He has contributed foundational work on <u>usability engineering</u> and the processes, skills, and tools required to deliver usable systems (see "Design for usability: Process, skills, and tools" (1999)). A major thread of his research models and measures *human performance* in fault diagnosis and supervisory control tasks, producing both experimental measures and computational models (e.g., "Measures of human problem solving performance in fault diagnosis tasks" (2012); "Development and evaluation of a model of human performance in a large-scale system" (2002)). Henneman has investigated the complexity of monitoring and controlling hierarchical, large-scale systems—developing metrics and simulation-based evaluations of operator workload and strategy ("On measuring the complexity of monitoring and controlling large-scale systems" (2007); related 1980s work on hierarchical networks). He also addresses applied HCI topics such as interface design guidelines for e-commerce, transaction-processing interfaces, handwriting-recognition system performance, and the organizational aspects of deploying human factors (e.g., "On the development of user interface design guidelines for electronic commerce" (1999); "On handwriting recognition system performance: Some experimental results" (1992); "Design of a human factors cost-justification tool" (1994)). In addition, he has engaged the HCI education community on curriculum and pedagogy for interaction design ("Design in the HCI classroom: Setting a research agenda" (2019); "The Master's Degree in HCI at 20" (2016)).

---

## Notable Works
- "Design for usability: Process, skills, and tools" (1999) — A widely cited synthesis that articulates practical approaches for integrating usability into system development, linking practitioner skills and tooling to measurable usability outcomes.
- "Measures of human problem solving performance in fault diagnosis tasks" (2012) — A rigorous review and empirical evaluation that identifies and validates a comprehensive set of performance measures for diagnosing faults in complex systems, informing both experimental practice and training assessment.
- "On measuring the complexity of monitoring and controlling large-scale systems" (2007) — A methodological contribution that develops and evaluates metrics (behavioral and structural) for assessing operator workload and system complexity in simulated large-scale networks.

---

## Academic Background
Henneman’s publication record spans from the early 1980s through the late 2010s, indicating a sustained academic career at the intersection of **human factors** and **HCI**. He is affiliated with Georgia Tech’s College of Computing and associated with the MSHCI program, and his work reflects both academic research and industry-facing projects (earlier collaborations and case studies involving NCR are documented in multiple papers). His contributions have been recognized in the field—accumulating over **440 total citations**—and include influential experimental studies, model development, applied design guidance, and contributions to HCI education. Henneman’s profile reflects a blend of empirical rigor, model-based reasoning, and practical engagement with usability practice and organizational adoption of human factors methods.

ptGYJiEAAAAJ.keywords: machine learning, computational and statistical neuroscience, Bayesian neural networks, variational inference, Gaussian processes, latent variable models, neural population dynamics, behavioral video analysis, inverse reinforcement learning, spatio-temporal brain connectivity
ptGYJiEAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Anqi Wu is an **Assistant Professor** in **Computational Science and Engineering** at **Georgia Tech**, where she leads a research program at the intersection of *machine learning* and *computational and statistical neuroscience*. Her lab develops principled probabilistic and deep-learning methods for neural and behavioral data with a strong emphasis on <u>uncertainty quantification</u>, <u>latent dynamics discovery</u>, and scalable <u>Gaussian process</u> models. Across methodological work on **Bayesian neural networks** and variational inference and applied studies of neural population activity and animal behavior, her group combines *theory-driven inference* with tools for large-scale neural and video datasets.

---

## Research Areas
Wu's research spans methodological advances in probabilistic machine learning and their application to neural systems and behavior. On the methodology side she has advanced variational inference for deep models—most prominently developing *deterministic variational inference* techniques for more robust **Bayesian neural networks**—and explored how gradient and Hessian information can improve Bayesian optimization and quadrature. She is a prolific contributor to the literature on <u>Gaussian processes</u>: developing GP-based nonlinear latent structure discovery for spike-train data, Markovian and multi-region GP formulations for scalable temporal and inter-regional brain communication modeling, and methods that integrate GPs with recurrent and state-space models to recover neural dynamics. On the neuroscience and behavioral side, her work includes principled *latent variable models* for neural population activity (e.g., the Neural Latents Benchmark '21), semi-supervised and graphical deep models for animal pose and behavioral video analysis (e.g., Deep Graph Pose; semi-supervised VAEs), and inverse reinforcement learning and reward-structure methods to characterize animal and robot behaviors. Across these topics she frequently couples structured probabilistic assumptions (graphical models, structured priors, region-sparse penalties) with deep learning and scalable inference to tackle high-dimensional, noisy experimental data.

---

## Notable Works
- "Deterministic variational inference for robust bayesian neural networks" (2018, 276 citations) — Introduced deterministic approximations and techniques that substantially improve stability and uncertainty calibration in variational Bayesian treatments of deep networks, making Bayesian deep learning more robust and practical for downstream tasks.
- "Gaussian process based nonlinear latent structure discovery in multivariate spike train data" (2017, 153 citations) — Developed a doubly nonlinear latent variable model that leverages GPs to extract low-dimensional, nonlinear latent structure from multi-neuron spike trains, enabling richer interpretations of population activity than linear dynamical approaches.
- "Neural latents benchmark'21: evaluating latent variable models of neural population activity" (2021, 142 citations) — Co-led a community benchmark that rigorously evaluates latent variable models on neural data, shaping standards for performance assessment and driving methodological progress in latent dynamics inference.

---

## Academic Background
Anqi Wu's publication record traces a sustained trajectory from early work on time-series and distance-based classification through to contemporary contributions in probabilistic deep learning and computational neuroscience. As an assistant professor at Georgia Tech she conducts interdisciplinary research that bridges machine learning methodology and experimental neuroscience, collaborating with labs focused on neural recording, behavior quantification, and robotic learning. Her work has accrued over **1,298 citations**, reflecting impact across machine learning, neuroscience, and applied domains (from Bayesian neural networks and Gaussian processes to pose estimation and inverse reinforcement learning). She maintains an active program of research, mentoring students and postdocs, and disseminates tools and evaluations that have influenced both theory and practice in neural data analysis.

puNJcv0AAAAJ.keywords: Human-Computer Interaction, Computer-Supported Cooperative Work, Autism Spectrum Disorders, Neurodiversity in the Workplace, Virtual Reality, Physiological Sensing (Electrodermal Activity), Assistive and Accessibility Technologies, Medical Crowdfunding and Online Health Communities, Conversational Agents and LLM-based Chatbots, Visual Analytics for Multimodal Sensor Data
puNJcv0AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Jennifer G. Kim is a faculty researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** who leads an interdisciplinary research program focused on designing technologies that promote *inclusive, independent, and supported living and working experiences* for neurodivergent and medically vulnerable populations. Her lab/group centers work at the intersection of **Human–Computer Interaction (HCI)** and **Computer-Supported Cooperative Work (CSCW)**, bringing together methods from design, qualitative fieldwork, physiological sensing, and interactive systems engineering. Her work foregrounds <u>independence</u> and <u>social support</u> (e.g., social networks and circles for young adults with autism), explores <u>trust and credibility</u> in online health communities such as medical crowdfunding, and investigates the use of <u>virtual reality</u>, <u>physiological sensing</u>, and **AI-driven conversational agents** to scaffold real-world transitions and workplace inclusion.

---

## Research Areas
Jennifer Kim’s research spans several tightly connected areas within HCI and CSCW. A major thread is the design of social technologies to support transitions to adulthood and employment for autistic and neurodivergent people (e.g., SocialMirror, circle-based SNS studies and transition-planning work). She investigates embodied and sensor-enabled methods—such as skin conductance and multimodal sensor visualization tools (BEDA, V-DAT, VISTA)—to measure and reflect emotional arousal and support *self-awareness* and training in situ. Another strand interrogates online health and support ecosystems, studying the social dynamics, credibility cues, and non-monetary contributions that shape success on medical crowdfunding platforms (works on collective endorsements, social translucence, and identity presentation). Kim also contributes to recommender and personalization research in academic and practical settings (personalized paper recommendation systems), and more recently to inclusive workplace technologies using immersive analytics and VR training to foster mutual understanding between neurotypical and neurodivergent coworkers. Emerging projects in her portfolio examine the opportunities and risks of LLM-driven chatbots and AI tools for sensitive domains (e.g., job-seeking support, eating-disorder recovery), and the design implications of social robots and augmented reality for daily-life support.

---

## Notable Works
- Designing social networks to support independence for young adults with autism — "Designing a social network to support the independence of young adults with autism" (2012). This formative design and field study established core design principles for leveraging trusted social ties and online tools to scaffold daily-life independence for autistic young adults (118 citations).
- Credibility and collective endorsement in health crowdfunding — "The power of collective endorsements: credibility factors in medical crowdfunding campaigns" (2016). This work identifies how endorsements, community signals, and presentation strategies shape donor decisions and community participation on medical fundraising platforms (90 citations).
- Personalized recommendation and research discovery — "Personalized academic research paper recommendation system" (2013). A systems-oriented contribution that combines user modeling and social signals to improve discovery of relevant scholarly literature, addressing information overload in academic publishing (83 citations).

---

## Academic Background
Jennifer Kim’s publication record (spanning highly cited conference and journal papers from 2011 onward) places her in the core HCI/CSCW research community, with sustained contributions to design-driven, interdisciplinary scholarship. Affiliated with the Georgia Institute of Technology, she collaborates across accessibility, health informatics, and immersive-systems communities and maintains an active lab website (http://jennifergahee.com/) that documents her projects and students. Her work has accumulated 798 citations to date, reflecting impact across both applied design interventions (neurodiversity, transition planning, workplace inclusion) and theory-building in online communities and sensing-driven interaction. She is regularly published at top HCI venues, engages in co-design and participatory methods with stakeholders (families, caregivers, clinicians, employers), and her trajectory indicates sustained engagement with grant-funded, translational research and interdisciplinary partnerships.

q_K8iFQAAAAJ.keywords: Cognitive Systems, Artificial Intelligence in Education, Human-AI Teaming, Concept Formation, Intelligent Tutoring Systems, Tutor Authoring, Simulated Learners, Incremental Concept Learning, Knowledge Tracing, Educational Data Mining
q_K8iFQAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Christopher J. MacLellan is an **Assistant Professor** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where he leads a research group focused on the intersection of **Cognitive Systems**, **Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED)**, and *human-centered machine intelligence*. His program of work centers on building computational models and interactive systems that enable effective <u>human–AI teaming</u>, scalable <u>intelligent tutoring systems</u>, and robust <u>concept formation</u> mechanisms that support incremental, interpretable learning. Across theory-driven architectures such as the **Apprentice Learner** family and applied systems for tutor authoring and evaluation, his research aims to close the loop between cognitive theory, educational data, and deployable AI technologies.

---

## Research Areas
MacLellan’s research spans several tightly connected areas: (1) *Human–AI Teaming* and team science for mixed human–machine teams, exemplified by work that synthesizes team-science principles to improve machine agents’ teamwork competencies; (2) **AI in Education**, with a sustained emphasis on automated tutor authoring, simulated learners, and methods for generating personalized feedback—manifest in contributions such as the *Apprentice Learner* architecture, SimStudent evaluations, and recent explorations of LLM-driven tutor feedback and apprentice-based authoring platforms; (3) *Computational models of learning and concept formation*, including probabilistic and incremental concept-formation algorithms (e.g., TRESTLE, Cobweb-derived approaches, and Cobweb4V) that address continual learning and catastrophic forgetting for both symbolic and visual domains; and (4) *analytics for open-ended and game-based learning*, involving replay analysis, conceptual feature extraction, and alignment-driven design methods to understand how rich interaction spaces map to learning outcomes. Recent work also engages interactive task learning with modern dialog models (e.g., Val) and new tutor frameworks (HTN-based tutors), bridging classic cognitive-modeling approaches with contemporary large-model capabilities.

---

## Notable Works
- "Improving teamwork competencies in human-machine teams: Perspectives from team science" (2021) — A perspective paper synthesizing team-science principles with AI techniques to chart directions for improving agent behavior, coordination, and evaluative frameworks in *human–machine teaming*; influential for framing interdisciplinary research on team competence in HMT.
- "Domain-general tutor authoring with apprentice learner models" (2022) — Demonstrates how *apprentice learner* models can underpin domain-general tutor authoring tools, reducing the burden of ITS construction and enabling educator-driven creation of personalized tutors.
- "The Apprentice Learner Architecture: Closing the loop between learning theory and educational data" (2016) — Introduces a unifying architecture that operationalizes learning theories as simulated learners to test hypotheses, automate tutor authoring, and connect cognitive models directly to educational data and adaptive instruction.

---

## Academic Background
MacLellan’s publication record dates back to the early 2010s and reveals a trajectory across cognitive science, educational technology, and AI research. He has contributed both foundational computational models (e.g., concept formation and simulated learners) and applied systems (authoring tools, tutor evaluation, and game analytics). Now based at Georgia Tech as an Assistant Professor, he leads an interdisciplinary agenda that combines cognitive modeling, machine learning, and human-centered design to address practical and theoretical problems in learning and teaming. His work has attracted broad attention within AIED and cognitive-systems communities (total citations ~974) and spans collaborations with researchers in team science, game-based learning, and human–computer interaction. His portfolio includes a mix of theory papers, architecture descriptions, system evaluations, and recent explorations integrating large language models into tutoring and interactive task learning.

vlV_GoEAAAAJ.keywords: Space, AI, Security, Edge Cloud, Distributed Systems, IoT-DDoS prevention, LEO satellite networks, Edge orchestration and scheduling, Edge ML serving (DNN model slicing), Lightweight edge containers
vlV_GoEAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Ketan Bhardwaj is a researcher in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he works at the intersection of systems, networking, and applied machine learning. His work centers on building practical, secure and scalable *edge-cloud* systems and novel compute platforms that span from mobile devices to Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites. Across his projects he emphasizes <u>edge computing</u>, <u>distributed systems</u>, <u>security for edge and IoT</u>, and the role of <u>AI/ML workloads at the edge</u>, producing systems and evaluations that move these ideas toward deployable infrastructure.

---

## Research Areas
Bhardwaj’s research portfolio covers a tightly connected set of topics in modern distributed systems and edge infrastructure. He has developed mechanisms for defending IoT and edge deployments against large-scale attacks (see "Towards {IoT-DDoS} prevention using edge computing"), and explored secure, scalable function onloading and isolation for edge functions in works such as "Fast, scalable and secure onloading of edge functions using airbox" and "SPX: Preserving end-to-end security for edge computing". He has advanced the performance and resource-efficiency of ML and visual analytics at constrained edge locations via model-slicing and serving systems ("Couper: Dnn model slicing for visual analytics containers at the edge", "Pocket: ml serving from the edge"). Bhardwaj has also pushed the boundary of how the edge extends into space: several recent papers investigate orchestration, routing, and scheduling for the emerging LEO satellite compute tier ("Toward Loosely Coupled Orchestration for the {LEO} Satellite Edge", "A characterization of route variability in LEO satellite networks", "Krios: Scheduling abstractions and mechanisms for enabling a LEO compute cloud"). Complementary work addresses practical edge problems — pre-rendered web delivery and accelerated browsing, lightweight container designs for resource-limited edge servers, edge-assisted XR and cloud gaming, and proposals for decentralized edge service discovery and DNS improvements ("Creation and delivery of pre-rendered web pages for accelerated browsing", "Toward lighter containers for the edge", "FleXR", "The Performance Argument for Blockchain-based Edge DNS Caching"). Together these papers reflect a systems-first approach that blends security, performance, and deployment realism.

---

## Notable Works
- "Towards {IoT-DDoS} prevention using edge computing" (2018) — A highly-cited study demonstrating how the edge can be used to mitigate application-level DDoS attacks launched by compromised IoT devices, combining traffic characterization with edge-based control mechanisms to make attacks harder to mount and easier to contain.
- "Fast, scalable and secure onloading of edge functions using airbox" (2016) — A systems contribution that proposes a back-end driven onloading approach (Airbox) for rapid, secure provisioning of edge functions, addressing latency, bandwidth, and isolation challenges for multi-tenant edge platforms.
- "Couper: Dnn model slicing for visual analytics containers at the edge" (2019) — Introduces model-slicing and container-level techniques to adapt DNN-based visual analytics to resource-constrained edge environments, enabling latency-sensitive vision workloads to run efficiently across heterogeneous edge deployments.

---

## Academic Background
Bhardwaj’s publication record spans a decade of systems and networking research (papers dating back to 2014) with a total citation count of 767, reflecting sustained influence in the edge-cloud and distributed systems communities. Based at Georgia Tech’s College of Computing, he has published on topics ranging from mobile web performance and personal cloud abstractions to cutting-edge research on the LEO satellite compute cloud and ML serving at the edge. His work appears in prominent workshops and conferences in mobile systems, edge/cloud computing, and networking (including HotMobile and SEC-related venues) and demonstrates collaborations across systems, security, and space-focused research communities. Further information, project pages, and copies of his papers are available from his academic homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~kbhardwa/

w55j5-8AAAAJ.keywords: Compilers, Embedded systems, Secure processors, Software debloating, Memory leak detection, Power and energy optimization, Parallel and distributed systems, Software transactional memory, Program partitioning, Anomaly/Intrusion detection
w55j5-8AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Santosh Pande is a **Professor of Computer Science** at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads an active research group working at the intersection of *compilers*, *architecture-aware systems*, and *systems security*. His work spans design and implementation of compiler and runtime techniques for **embedded and parallel systems**, as well as hardware–software approaches to improve *security* and *reliability*. Across these topics he emphasizes practical, deployable solutions that combine compiler analyses, microarchitectural support, and lightweight runtime mechanisms. His publications have amassed over **2476 citations**, reflecting broad impact on topics such as <u>information leakage mitigation</u>, <u>software debloating</u>, and <u>automated memory-leak detection</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Pande’s research combines traditional and emerging compiler techniques with hardware and runtime support to address performance, energy, and security problems. In the area of *information flow and tamper-resistant execution* he developed infrastructure to mitigate <u>information leakage on the address bus</u> ("HIDE: an infrastructure for efficiently protecting information leakage on the address bus", 2004) and explored related partitioning techniques ("Tamper-resistant whole program partitioning", 2003; "Leakage-proof program partitioning", 2002). For embedded and DSP processors he has advanced register allocation and storage assignment techniques to generate compact, efficient code ("Storage assignment optimizations...", 1999; "Storage assignment through variable coalescence", 2003; "Hardware-managed register allocation for embedded processors", 2004), and proposed compiler–microarchitecture co-designs to reduce static power dissipation by gating idle functional units ("Optimizing static power dissipation...", 2002). In systems security and software hardening he has worked on *control-flow obfuscation* and hardware-assisted protection for embedded devices ("Hardware assisted control flow obfuscation for embedded processors", 2004), anomaly detection at the control-flow level and data-protection mechanisms ("Hardware supported anomaly detection...", 2004; "Using branch correlation to identify infeasible paths for anomaly detection", 2006). More recently his group has contributed to practical software hardening and attack-surface reduction via *software debloating* ("Blankit library debloating...", 2020; "Carve: Practical security-focused software debloating...", 2019; "Is less really more?..." 2019) and to production-ready tooling for defect detection such as automated memory-leak detection ("Automated memory leak detection for production use" / Sniper, 2014) and ML-driven introspective leak detection ("Detecting memory leaks through introspective dynamic behavior modelling...", 2014). He has also produced influential work on compiler support for parallel and distributed systems, including scheduling and compilation frameworks for functional parallelism and multi-GPU systems ("Compilation techniques for parallel systems", 1999; "A scalable scheduling scheme...", 2002; "Case: A compiler-assisted scheduling framework for multi-gpu systems", 2022).

---

## Notable Works
- "HIDE: an infrastructure for efficiently protecting information leakage on the address bus" (2004) — introduced a practical infrastructure to mitigate <u>address-bus information leakage</u> for secure processors, combining hardware and software techniques to protect confidentiality in adversarial deployment settings; this work is a cornerstone for later efforts on leakage-resistant partitioning and secure execution on constrained devices.
- "Brainy: Effective selection of data structures" (2011) — advanced compiler- and architecture-aware methods for automated selection of data structures, demonstrating how cross-layer analysis (application semantics + hardware behavior) can yield large performance improvements and better programmer productivity.
- "Automated memory leak detection for production use" (Sniper, 2014) — delivered a low-overhead, sampling-based leak detection tool for C/C++ production code, making memory-leak detection practical in deployed software and spawning follow-on research in staleness-based and ML-driven leak identification.

## Academic Background
Santosh Pande has a long-standing academic career in systems, compilers, and security research dating back to the 1990s and is a senior faculty member in the Georgia Tech Computer Science community. Over three decades he has authored and co-authored numerous widely cited papers across compiler optimizations, embedded-system code generation, parallelization, and security-aware systems research. His sustained publication record (including highly cited works from 1994 through the 2020s) and broad collaborative footprint reflect leadership in cross-disciplinary research that brings together compiler design, microarchitecture, and practical security. At Georgia Tech he directs graduate and doctoral research, collaborates with industry and government sponsors on deployable systems, and mentors students who go on to research and engineering careers in programming languages, compilers, and systems security.

xY3zIEAAAAAJ.keywords: sonification, auditory displays, human-computer interaction, driving, psychology, auditory menus, spatialized audio navigation, auditory graphs, psychophysical scaling, trust in automated driving
xY3zIEAAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Bruce N. Walker is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** who curates the Sonify research web site and works at the intersection of **psychology**, **human–computer interaction**, and auditory display research. His work centers on the design, evaluation, and theory of *sonification* and *auditory interfaces*, with applied programs in driving and safety, wearable and mobile audio navigation, and multimodal in-vehicle systems. Walker’s portfolio emphasizes <u>data-to-sound mappings</u>, <u>auditory menu and beacon design</u>, and <u>human factors of automation and trust in driving</u>, linking perceptual theory to practical toolkits and field-relevant studies.

---

## Research Areas
Walker’s research spans foundational theory, perceptual measurement, system/tool development, and applied evaluation. He has helped to define the field of sonification through major syntheses and research agendas (e.g., "Sonification report: Status of the field and research agenda", 2010) and formal theoretical treatments (e.g., "Theory of sonification", 2011). Empirical work on perceptual mappings and psychophysical scaling (e.g., "Psychophysical scaling of sonification mappings", 2000; "Magnitude estimation of conceptual data dimensions", 2002) informs practical design guidance for auditory graphs and sonification toolkits such as the Sonification Sandbox (2003, 2007). Walker has also advanced applied auditory interface design—developing and evaluating cues like *spearcons* and other speech-based earcons to improve menu navigation (e.g., "Spearcons (speech-based earcons) improve navigation performance...", 2013; "Learnability of sound cues...", 2008)—and investigated wearable and spatialized audio for wayfinding (e.g., "Swan: System for wearable audio navigation", 2007; "Navigation performance with a virtual auditory display", 2006). More recently his work extends into transportation safety, affect and driver behavior, and trust in automation (e.g., STS-AD, 2020; studies of emotion effects on driving, 2011–2015), and into cross-domain applications such as UAVs for construction safety and multimodal diagnostic tools that combine visual and sonified outputs.

---

## Notable Works
- "Sonification report: Status of the field and research agenda" (2010) — a widely cited synthesis that maps the landscape of sonification research, sets a research agenda, and has served as a canonical reference for both theoretical and applied sonification work.
- "Spearcons (speech-based earcons) improve navigation performance in advanced auditory menus" (2013) — design and evaluation of hybrid speech-like cues that demonstrably enhance auditory menu navigation and learning, influencing auditory interface design for mobile and assistive technologies.
- "Situational trust scale for automated driving (STS-AD): Development and initial validation" (2020) — development and validation of a psychometric scale to measure drivers’ trust in automated driving systems, contributing tools for assessing human–automation interaction and safety-critical adoption.

---

## Academic Background
Walker’s publication record (total citations ~9,768) reflects a long-standing, interdisciplinary program bridging **psychology**, **auditory perception**, and **human–computer interaction**. His online presence at the Sonify site (http://sonify.psych.gatech.edu/~walkerb/) and sustained collaborations across HCI, transportation, and accessibility research indicate deep ties to Georgia Tech’s psychology and engineering communities and to applied research consortia (e.g., IVAT/in-vehicle assistive technology projects). Over two decades he has produced influential methodological contributions (toolkits, psychophysical scaling methods), theoretical syntheses, and applied evaluations that have been adopted by researchers and practitioners in accessibility, automotive human factors, and construction safety (UAS/UAV applications). Walker’s body of work has been supported by competitive research funding and recognized through high citation impact and widespread use of his design artifacts (e.g., Sonification Sandbox), reflecting both scholarly influence and practical deployment across domains.

xkCYK58AAAAJ.keywords: Cybersecurity, distributed systems, causal consistency, distributed shared memory, replication and quorum systems, distributed operating systems, context-aware security, malware analysis and detection, telephony security, botnet C&C detection
xkCYK58AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Mustaque Ahamad is a senior faculty researcher and professor at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he conducts long-standing work at the intersection of **distributed systems** and **cybersecurity**. He leads and collaborates with research groups that span systems design, security analytics, and applied measurement studies. His work focuses on building principled, scalable systems and security mechanisms that balance *consistency, performance, and trust* — with particular emphasis on <u>weak consistency models for distributed shared memory</u>, <u>context-aware access control</u>, and <u>measurement-driven defenses for networked and telephony threats</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Ahamad’s research covers two tightly connected families of problems. In distributed systems, he has shaped thinking around memory and consistency models (for example, “Causal memory: Definitions, implementation, and programming” (1995) and “Slow memory: Weakening consistency to enhance concurrency in distributed shared memories” (1990)), scalable replication and quorum protocols (“The grid protocol: A high performance scheme for maintaining replicated data” (1992); “Performance characterization of quorum-consensus algorithms for replicated data” (2002)), and efficient logical clock constructions (e.g., “Plausible clocks: constant size logical clocks for distributed systems” (1999)). In security and applied measurement, his work spans access control for pervasive environments (“Securing context-aware applications using environment roles” (2001); “A context-aware security architecture for emerging applications” (2002); “Generalized role-based access control for securing future applications” (2000)), web and network malware measurement and mitigation (“WebWitness: investigating, categorizing, and mitigating malware download paths” (2015); “ExecScent: Mining for new C&C domains in live networks” (2013)), and telephony/voice security and fraud detection (“Phoneypot: Data-driven understanding of telephony threats.” (2015); “Pindr0p: Using single-ended audio features to determine call provenance” (2010); “Callrank: Combating SPIT using call duration, social networks and global reputation” (2007)). Methodologically, his body of work mixes formal modeling and systems-building with empirical measurement, visualization, and machine learning techniques (e.g., video anomaly detection with hierarchical representations (2015) and recent counter-misinformation response generation).

---

## Notable Works
- "Causal memory: Definitions, implementation, and programming" (1995) — formalized and implemented a family of *causal memory* models that trade strict global ordering for increased concurrency and scalability; a foundational contribution to weak consistency semantics in distributed shared memory systems.
- "Securing context-aware applications using environment roles" (2001) — introduced practical mechanisms and architectural ideas for applying *context* and environmental roles to access control in pervasive and ubiquitous computing, influencing later work on context-aware security and role-based access control extensions.
- "The grid protocol: A high performance scheme for maintaining replicated data" (1992) — proposed a logical-grid organization and replication protocol that advanced high-availability, low-latency approaches to maintaining replicated state in wide-area distributed systems.

---

## Academic Background
Mustaque Ahamad has a multi-decade academic career bridging core distributed systems theory and applied security research. With influential publications dating from the late 1980s onward, he has developed both foundational models (memory consistency, logical clocks, quorum protocols) and a broad portfolio of applied security projects (malware measurement, telephony fraud, SSL/TLS measurement, and defenses). His work has been widely cited (total citations ~9,630), reflecting impact across systems and security communities. At Georgia Tech he has supervised graduate students, led multi-institution projects, and served on program committees and editorial venues consistent with a senior scholar in the field. He has been supported by major research grants and is active in professional societies (ACM/IEEE venues), contributing to both theoretical advances and deployed tools that address real-world security and scalability challenges.

yaCigtkAAAAJ.keywords: Educational Technology, Learning at Scale, AI in Education, Online Education, Computer Science Education, Conversational AI, Instructional Video Design, Scalable Assessment and Feedback, Academic Integrity and Plagiarism Detection, Affective Computing and Sentiment Analysis
yaCigtkAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
David A. Joyner is a faculty member in the School of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and a prominent researcher and practitioner in *educational technology* and *computer science education at scale*. He has played a central role in research and operational efforts around Georgia Tech’s online and MOOC-based programs, contributing to course design, platform affordances, and policy for large-scale credit-bearing degrees. Joyner’s work centers on the design and evaluation of <u>AI-augmented educational systems</u> (including conversational agents and intelligent tutoring agents), the pedagogy and production of instructional media, and the organizational practices that enable <u>teaching and assessment at scale</u>. His scholarship blends empirical studies, system-building, and theoretical framing to address problems of access, quality, and integrity in online learning.

---

## Research Areas
Joyner’s research spans several interlocking areas within *learning at scale* and *AI in education*. He investigates how conversational agents and virtual teaching assistants function socially and pedagogically (e.g., “Towards mutual theory of mind in human-ai interaction…” and “Jill Watson SA”), and he develops frameworks for designing video-based instruction and multimedia lessons for online learners (“Designing and developing video lessons for online learning: A seven-principle model.”; “Designing videos with pedagogical strategies”). A recurring theme is assessment and academic integrity at scale: he studies scalable feedback systems, anti-plagiarism tooling (e.g., TAPS and MOSS extensions), and alternatives to digital proctoring (“Scaling expert feedback: Two case studies”; “TAPS: A MOSS extension…”; “On the necessity (or lack thereof) of digital proctoring”). Joyner also examines program-level questions—how to build, govern, and sustain affordable, credit-bearing online degrees—through empirical analyses of the OMSCS program and MOOC-for-credit efforts (“Master’s at scale: Five years…”, “Components of assessments and grading at scale”, “Ten years, ten trends…”). His work brings together learning sciences, human-computer interaction, and practical systems engineering to improve learning outcomes and learner experience in large, distributed classrooms.

## Notable Works
- “Towards mutual theory of mind in human-ai interaction: How language reflects what students perceive about a virtual teaching assistant” (2021) — Introduced and empirically examined the idea of *Mutual Theory of Mind* as a design lens for long-term, community-facing conversational agents, connecting language use to users’ mental models of virtual instructors.  
- “Designing and developing video lessons for online learning: A seven-principle model.” (2019) — Proposed and validated a seven-principle framework for producing effective instructional videos for online courses, combining production practice with learner-centered pedagogy and measurement of perceived effectiveness.  
- “Using AI to teach AI: Lessons from an online AI class” (2017) — Reported on the design, scaling, and pedagogical outcomes of offering a foundational AI course in Georgia Tech’s online MSCS program, detailing how AI techniques and learning-science principles can be combined to teach AI to large cohorts.

## Academic Background
Joyner’s scholarship is deeply tied to Georgia Tech’s efforts to create high-quality, affordable graduate computing education at scale; his papers document both course-level experiments (e.g., CS7637 Knowledge-Based AI / Cognitive Systems) and program-level analyses of the OMSCS/online-degree ecosystem. He collaborates across disciplinary lines with learning scientists and computing researchers (e.g., work on ecosystem modeling and middle-school science learning with the ACT project) and contributes to tooling and policy for scalable instruction (anti-plagiarism systems, grading workflows, and semi-synchronous lecture platforms). With a research footprint reflected in over 2,000 citations and a sustained publication record on topics from conversational AI to assessment design, Joyner is recognized for translating research into operational practices that influence how large online courses and degrees are designed, delivered, and governed. More about his courses, projects, and publications is available on his homepage: http://www.davidjoyner.net/.

zh8E4voAAAAJ.keywords: Human-Computer Interaction, Digital Health, Health Equity, Health Disparities, Social Computing, mHealth, AI-generated misinformation, Crisis Informatics, Culturally tailored health interventions, Family-centered health technologies
zh8E4voAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Andrea G. Parker is an **Associate Professor** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where she leads a research group within the **College of Computing** focused on the intersection of technology, health, and social justice. Her work sits at the nexus of **Human–Computer Interaction (HCI)**, **Digital Health**, and **Social Computing**, and is oriented toward designing technology that addresses <u>health inequities</u> and amplifies community-centered health practices. Across empirical, design, and systems-oriented projects, Parker investigates how socio-technical systems — from mobile health apps and wearable trackers to AI-driven information ecosystems — shape health behaviors, access to care, and public understanding of health information.

---

## Research Areas
Parker’s research spans multiple, tightly interwoven areas of HCI and public health. She investigates design and evaluation of *mHealth interventions* for vulnerable and low-SES communities (e.g., EatWell, family-focused reflective systems, and wearable activity-tracking studies), exploring how culturally grounded design and collective reflection can improve engagement and outcomes. Her work on *celebratory technology* reframes health technology away from solely corrective approaches toward systems that celebrate cultural practices around food and wellbeing. More recently, she has examined the sociotechnical dynamics of information integrity and trust — including the production and mitigation of **AI-generated misinformation** (Synthetic lies) and systematic studies of trust in social platforms and COVID-19 crisis visualizations. Other threads include community- and faith-based interventions, culturally tailored virtual agents, perinatal and women’s mental health in marginalized groups, and the use of speculative and activist design methods (e.g., afrofuturist toolkits) to interrogate power, representation, and equity in design.

---

## Notable Works
- "Synthetic lies: Understanding ai-generated misinformation and evaluating algorithmic and human solutions" (2023) — a leading empirical and evaluative study on how large language models can generate persuasive misinformation and what algorithmic and human-centered interventions are effective at detection and mitigation.  
- "Celebratory technology: new directions for food research in HCI" (2008) — a foundational piece that reframes digital food and nutrition research toward approaches that honor cultural practices and social meanings of food rather than purely corrective health messaging.  
- "Designing and evaluating mHealth interventions for vulnerable populations: A systematic review" (2018) — a comprehensive synthesis of mHealth work that highlights design challenges, equity gaps, and evidence-based strategies for developing interventions tailored to underserved and vulnerable groups.

---

## Academic Background
Andrea G. Parker is an established HCI researcher embedded in Georgia Tech’s College of Computing as an Associate Professor. Trained in interdisciplinary methods that bridge computing, design, and public health, she has developed a sustained program of community-engaged, equity-centered research reflected in >4,600 citations to date. Her scholarship appears across HCI, CSCW, and health informatics venues and demonstrates long-term collaborations with community organizations, faith-based institutions, and public-health partners. Parker’s portfolio includes mixed-methods studies, participatory design projects, systematic reviews, and design artifacts that inform both academic debate and practical intervention. Her work has attracted competitive funding and attention from the HCI and public health communities and has contributed to shaping conversations about technology’s role in addressing health disparities, crisis communication, and culturally responsive design.

zp8V7ZMAAAAJ.keywords: Robotic Manipulation, Robot Learning, Reinforcement Learning, Machine Learning, Computer Vision, Large Language Models, Multimodal Perception, Imitation Learning, Sim-to-Real Transfer, Contact-Rich Manipulation
